question: Seven employees' annual salaries, in thousands, are 32, 35, 38, 40, 42, 45, and 220. Find the MEDIAN salary (in thousands).
hints: The median is the middle value once the data are ordered - these already are.
With 7 values, the middle is the 4th one; the huge 220 does not change which value sits in the middle.
explanation: The median is the 4th of 7 ordered values, 40. Note the mean would be 452/7 = 64.6, dragged far up by the single 220 - which is exactly why the median is the honest 'typical' salary here.

question: For each situation, which measure of central tendency best represents the 'typical' value? Classify each row.

rows.label: Favorite ice-cream flavor reported by 300 children (chocolate, vanilla, ...)
Annual incomes in a town where three billionaires live among modest earners
Heights of 500 adult women, roughly symmetric with no extremes
Marathon finish times, stretched by a few very slow finishers

hints: Categorical (non-numeric) data leaves only one usable measure; skew or outliers rule out another.
Flavors -> mode; billionaire-skewed income -> median; symmetric heights -> mean; a few slow finishers -> median.
explanation: Categorical flavors admit only the mode; skewed income and outlier-stretched finish times call for the outlier-robust median; symmetric height data is the one case where the mean is safe. Defaulting to the mean without checking for skew is the classic error.

question: You add one extreme outlier to a data set. Sort each statistic by how much that single outlier moves it.
hints: Ask which statistics use every value's exact size (or the extremes) versus only position or frequency.
Mean, range, and SD all feed on the actual magnitudes; median and mode depend on position and frequency.
explanation: The mean, range, and standard deviation all incorporate the outlier's raw magnitude, so they shift strongly; the median (a position) and mode (a frequency) barely move. This robustness split is the whole reason we ever prefer the median.

question: A student summarizes descriptive statistics. Highlight the one incorrect statement.

tokens.text: The mean is the sum of all values divided by the count.
The median is the middle value once the data are ordered.
Because it uses every value, the mean is more robust to outliers than the median.
The mode is the only central-tendency measure usable for purely categorical data.
Standard deviation expresses spread in the data's original units.

hints: Four statements are correct; one reverses which measure resists outliers.
Using every value is exactly why the mean is DRAGGED by an extreme - so it is less robust, not more.
explanation: The error is t3. Using every value makes the mean SENSITIVE to outliers, not robust to them; the median, which ignores the magnitudes of extremes, is the outlier-resistant one. This reversal is the lesson's central pitfall.

question: Choose the more representative summary and the reason.
segments: To describe the 'typical' home price in a neighborhood where one 10-million-dollar mansion sits among otherwise modest houses, you should report the
wordBank: median, because it is barely moved by the single extreme value
mean, because it uses every value including the mansion
range, because it captures the full spread of prices
mode, because house prices are categorical
hints: 'Typical' means representative of most homes, so the lone mansion should not dominate.
The measure that ignores the magnitude of the one extreme is the median.
explanation: The median resists the mansion's pull and represents what most homes actually cost; the mean would be inflated by the single extreme, the range only measures spread, and prices are numeric (not categorical), so the mode is inappropriate.
